coredata.dc:title: Gold–carbon dot (Au@Cd) nanoconjugates based electrochemical sensing of cholesterol and effect of nitrogen ion implantation on sensitivity
coredata.dc:creator: Priyadarshini, Eepsita
Pandey, Saurabh
Rawat, Kamla
coredata.dc:description: Serum cholesterol dysregulation is associated with prognosis and diagnosis of many diseases and effective biosensor will improvise their management. A novel electrochemical biosensor was fabricated based on gelatin-Au@CD nanoconjugate films for cholesterol detection. Initially, the surface of indium titanium oxide (ITO) coated glass was modified by drop casting of gelatin-Au@CD nanoconjugates to prepare the electrodes. Electrochemical studies for detection of bioanalytes(such as urea (U), ascorbic acid (AA), oxalic acid (OA), gallic acid (GA), cholesterol (Chox), dextrose (D), l-cysteine (Cys) and citric acid (CA)) were performed using cyclic voltammetry. The presence of nanoconjugates provided an appropriate environment for enhanced electrochemical response for cholesterol. These electrodes exhibited a linear response towards the presence of cholesterol in the linear concentration range of 2–20 mM with a correlation coefficient of 0.95, and the superior sensitivity of 1.36 μA/mM/cm2. Additionally, enhanced sensitivity (2.99 μA/mM/cm2) of nitrogen ion irradiated films up to a fluence of 1016 ions/cm2 was noticed because of morphological changes in the electrode surface brought about by irradiation. Approximately 54% enhancement was found when the ion fluence was 1016 ions/cm2. The designed nanoconjugate electrode showed excellent response towards cholesterol sensing and eliminates the requirement of any enzymes making the overall process simpler, cost-effective and allows for room temperature storage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Au@CD nanoconjugates
Electrochemical sensing
Ion implantation
Enzyme-free sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots and Au@CD nanoconjugates
Characterization of non-irradiated and irradiated gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode
Effect of nitrogen (N2 +) beam implantation on cholesterol detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: KR acknowledges ICMR ( 35/11/2019-Nano/BMS ) and UGC-startup, Government of India, for research grant. The authors thank Advanced Instrument Research Facility (AIRF) of the University for TEM, SEM and FTIR facility. We are thankful to the Inter University Accelerator Centre (IUAC) for nitrogen ion beam irradiation facility. The authors are thankful to Dr. K. Asokan, IUAC for useful discussions.

KR acknowledges ICMR (35/11/2019-Nano/BMS) and UGC-startup, Government of India, for research grant. The authors thank Advanced Instrument Research Facility (AIRF) of the University for TEM, SEM and FTIR facility. We are thankful to the Inter University Accelerator Centre (IUAC) for nitrogen ion beam irradiation facility. The authors are thankful to Dr. K. Asokan, IUAC for useful discussions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of synthesized Au@CDnanoconjugates a) UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectra b) TEM image.

a) Electrochemical behavior of gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode as a function of Au@CD nanoconjugate concentration. Inset shows the variation in the anodic and cathodic current with respect to Au@CD nanoconjugate concentration. Note maximum sensitivity was observed at 0.2% (w/v) concentration. b) Electrochemical behavior of gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode with different scan rates varying from 10 to 100 mV/s. Inset shows the variation in peak current (anodic and cathodic) as a function of square root of scan rate. c) Electrochemical behavior of gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode on treatment with varying cholesterol concentration. d) Linear dependence of peak current as a function of cholesterol concentration (2–10 mM) showing the sensitivity of the system towards cholesterol.

Graph showing the selectivity of the Au@CDs/gelatin ITO electrode for cholesterol. Note: There was no significant variation in the peak current for different analytes with increasing cholesterol concentration except for cholesterol (increase in current form 400–520 μA). Graphical illustration of the mechanism of electrochemical sensing of cholesterol by Gelatin/Au@CD-ITO.

a) CV graphs of the Au@CDs ITO slides irradiated at different N2+ ion fluence b) Graph showing the anodic and cathodic peak current for gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode subjected to varied N2 + ions ion fluencies. Cyclic voltammagraphs of (c) anodic (d) cathodic peak current on treatment with varying concentration of cholesterol (2–20 mM) for irradiated gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode of different fluence.

Sensitivity of gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode irradiated at different fluence. Note the maximum sensitivity was observed for electrode irradiated at the fluence of 1016 ions/cm2 (Upper Panel). b) SEM images of non-irradiated and irradiated gelatin/Au@CD- ITO elctrodes a) non irradiated electrode b) non irradiated electrode on treatment with cholesterol c) irradiate with N2 + ion beam of fluence 5 × 1014 d) 5 × 1014 per cm2 irradiated electrode after treatment with cholesterole) 1016 ions per cm2 f) 1016 ions per cm2 irradiated electrode after treatment with cholesterol (Lower Panel).

Summary of performance of different sensors in the detection of cholesterol. This table has been adapted from our previous study (Priyadarshini E and Rawat K, 2017; ref. [13]), the Au@CDs nanoconjugates were observed to be highly selective towards cholesterol, in the presence of several interfering analytes.

row.entry: ZnO NPs immobilized with cholesterol oxidase
Magnetic nanoparticles with glucose oxidase (GOx)/cholesterol oxidase (ChOx)

head.ce:title: Gold–carbon dot (Au@Cd) nanoconjugates based electrochemical sensing of cholesterol and effect of nitrogen ion implantation on sensitivity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Sciences, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Sciences, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, School of Chemical and Life Sciences, Jamia Hamdard, New Delhi, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Life Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biochemistry, School of Chemical and Life Sciences, Jamia Hamdard, New Delhi, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Chemical and Life Sciences, Jamia Hamdard, New Delhi, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Life Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Chemical and Life Sciences, Jamia Hamdard, New Delhi, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Serum cholesterol dysregulation is associated with prognosis and diagnosis of many diseases and effective biosensor will improvise their management. A novel electrochemical biosensor was fabricated based on gelatin-Au@CD nanoconjugate films for cholesterol detection. Initially, the surface of indium titanium oxide (ITO) coated glass was modified by drop casting of gelatin-Au@CD nanoconjugates to prepare the electrodes. Electrochemical studies for detection of bioanalytes(such as urea (U), ascorbic acid (AA), oxalic acid (OA), gallic acid (GA), cholesterol (Chox), dextrose (D), l-cysteine (Cys) and citric acid (CA)) were performed using cyclic voltammetry. The presence of nanoconjugates provided an appropriate environment for enhanced electrochemical response for cholesterol. These electrodes exhibited a linear response towards the presence of cholesterol in the linear concentration range of 2–20 mM with a correlation coefficient of 0.95, and the superior sensitivity of 1.36 μA/mM/cm2. Additionally, enhanced sensitivity (2.99 μA/mM/cm2) of nitrogen ion irradiated films up to a fluence of 1016 ions/cm2 was noticed because of morphological changes in the electrode surface brought about by irradiation. Approximately 54% enhancement was found when the ion fluence was 1016 ions/cm2. The designed nanoconjugate electrode showed excellent response towards cholesterol sensing and eliminates the requirement of any enzymes making the overall process simpler, cost-effective and allows for room temperature storage.

ce:section.ce:para: Cholesterol forms an essential structural component of the membranes and is the precursor in the biosynthesis of various steroids and hormones. Besides this it found in tissues, skin, brain, liver and fatty sheets of the body. In human body cholesterol generally occurs in two different forms, cholesterol ester (esterified form) and free-cholesterol. The normal concentration of cholesterol in human serum should be in the range of 2.86–5.98 mM for healthy individual. Therefore, its optimum level of concentration is quite crucial because deregulation in cholesterol concentration is associated with several diseases. High concentration of cholesterol often leads to hypertension, cardiovascular disorders, brain thrombosis and lipid dysfunction [1]. Cholesterol below the optimum concentration is correlated to anemia, malnutrition and hyperthyrea syndromes [2] (see Table 1 ).

It is therefore essential to estimate the level of cholesterol in human blood and has become an important bio-marker for the diagnosis of heart disease, brain thrombosis and so on [3,4]. Mostly, enzyme (cholesterol oxidase) based methods are used for estimation of cholesterol due to its specificity and rapid analysis potential [5]. However, these methods require maintenance of sterile and stringent environment conditions in addition to being costly. Apart from this, fabrication of enzymes onto an appropriate matrix along with retention of properties is crucial. For this new matrices that avoid the bioleaching of enzymes are being investigated. Basically, the efficiency of a biosensor (selectivity, sensitivity and stability) depends on the use of appropriate matrix for immobilization. Metal nanoparticles and quantum dots have been reported to exhibit enhanced electrocatalytic activity [6] and have shown a very high degree of stability [7]. Electrodes designed using carbon nanotubes (CNs) show superior electron transfer with the capability to easily oxidize biomolecules [8–10]. Nanohybrids using a combination of gold nanoparticles (GNP) and CNs have also been used for their superior electrocatalytic properties [11,12].

Our earlier studies [13] reported the potential capability of Au@CD nanoconjugates towards selective detection of cholesterol. Earlier, we had focused on a dual mode detection of cholesterol using both UV–visible spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. As an extension to the study, the current investigation focuses on determining the electrochemical changes brought about by cholesterol on treatment with gelatin-Au@CD/ITO electrode. Gopalan et al. [14] have demonstrated the use of GNP distributed MWNT, Chitosan biopolymer and ionic liquid based development of MWNT(SH)–Au/Chi–IL/ChOx electrode matrix for detection of cholesterol and obtained a sensitivity of 200 μ/AM. However, to the best of our knowledge, the combination of gelatin and Au@CD nanoconjugates as a matrix for detection analysis have not been used for construction of a biosensor for cholesterol.

In the current study, we have designed a novel kind of matrix wherein gelatin and Au@CD nanoconjugates are immobilized on to a ITO electrode. The developed electrode was then analyzed for its specificity for cholesterol detection via cyclic voltammetric analysis. Thus, we have fabricated an electrochemical sensor for cholesterol by taking the advantages specificity of Au@CD nanoconjugates towards cholesterol. Further, we have analyzed the effect of low-energy ion irradiation (50 KeV N2 + ion) on the sensitivity of cholesterol detection. As irradiation is known to induce changes in the morphological and electrochemical properties of the used electrode, we tried to establish a comparative analysis of the system by irradiating these electrodes with different N2 + ion fluences. A significant aspect of the present study involves its simplicity and non-enzymatic mode of detection that makes the approach facile and cost-effective.

All the materials and reagents were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Gelatin-B (bovine skin extract), 225 bloom with pI of ∼4.9 ± 0.2 and molecular weight 50 KDa was used for gelation. All other chemicals used were of analytical grade. Indium-tin-oxide (ITO) coated glass plates were obtained from Balzers, UK, (Baltracom 247 ITO, 1.1 mm thick) with a sheet resistance and transmittance of 25 Ω sq−1 and 90%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots and Au@CD nanoconjugates
ce:section.ce:para: We used citric acid and polyethylene glycol as the precursors for CD synthesis. HAuCl4 (0.06 mg ml−1) was added to the synthesized CDs and boiled to allow the generation of Au@CDs nanoconjugates. The detailed protocol of synthesis and characterization was as per the prior standardized procedure described by Priyadarshini et al. [13].

The gelatin/Au@CD thin films were prepared as follows. Gelatin solution was prepared by dissolving gelatin powder 2.5% (w/v) in deionized water and subjecting it to continuous stirring for 30 min at 40 °C. This results in the formation of a homogeneous and optically clear solution. Thereafter, Au@CDs nanoconjugates(0.2%) was added to the gelatin solution and stirred for 30 min at 40 °C to form gelatin/Au@CD nanoconjugate suspension.

Thin film of gelatin/Au@CDs-ITO electrode was prepared by drop casting method. Briefly, 40 μl of the suspension was dispersed onto ITO slides with a covered surface area of 0.25 cm2. The films were allowed to dry at room temperature (25 °C) for 24 h. We thereafter analyzed the electrochemical behavior of the prepared electrodes and maximum current was observed at Au@CD nanaoconjugate concentration of 0.2% (v/v). Except for a few experiments (standardization of Au@CD concentration) all other experiments were performed on thin films containing 2.5% (w/v) gelatin B and 0.2% (v/v) Au@CD nanoconjugates.

Electrochemical sensing of analytes was conducted via Cyclic voltammetry (CV) studies on a Autolab Potentiostat/Galvanostat (Eco Chemie, Netherlands) using a three electrode cell under the applied potential range of −0.05 to 0.5 V. The three electrode cell used was a working electrode (Gelatin/Au@CD-ITO), an auxiliary electrode that was a platinum wire and the reference electrode that was Ag/AgCl. The CV studies were conducted using Zobell's solution (3.3 mM K4Fe(CN)6, 3.3 mM K3Fe(CN)6 containing 0.1 M KCl at pH 7). Various parameters such as scan rate, Au@CD nanoconjugate and analyte concentration were standardized by repeating the experiments in duplicates under same experimental conditions.

The different analytes used in the study were urea (U), ascorbic acid (AA), oxalic acid (OA), gallic acid (GA), cholesterol (Chox), dextrose (D), l-cysteine (Cys) and citric acid (CA). Stock solutions of 20 mM of these analytes were separately prepared in appropriate solvent and stored at 4 °C when not in use.

To understand the effect of ion irradiation on sensing, the gelatin/Au@CD-ITO thin films were subjected to irradiation with 50 keV nitrogen molecular ion (N2 +) beam having different fluencies (1012 to 5 × 1016 ions/cm2) at the Inter-University Accelerator Centre (IUAC), New Delhi [15]. Electrochemical profiling of aforesaid analytes were performed using both gelatin/Au@CD-ITO and irradiated gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrodes.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was conducted on a RigakuD/Max 2200 powder X-ray diffractometer with a CuKα target (λ = 1.54439 Å) and a Peltier-cooled solid-state detector. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) imaging was carried out using SEM, Zeiss, EVO-40 instrument.

The synthesis of Au@CDs nanoconjugates was confirmed using various analytical characterization techniques which have been briefly described by Priyadarshini et al. [13]. The appearance of surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak 520–580 nm and a fluorescence peak at 454 nm on exciting at 334 nm confirmed the dual properties of synthesized particles. In this study, Au@CD nanoconjugates of 12.9 ± 1 nm particle size were used for electrochemical studies. Fig. 1 shows the physical characterization of the Au@CD nanoconjugate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of non-irradiated and irradiated gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode

ce:section.ce:para: XRD analysis of the irradiated and non-irradiated gelatin/Au@ CD-ITO electrode was done to determine any changes in the structural properties. Figure S2 (Supplementary Information) shows the XRD patterns of the electrodes with and without irradiation of 50 KeV N2 + ion beam. We observed broadening and increase in intensity of the characteristic peaks associated with Au@CDs nanoconjugates. This might be due to the increase Furthermore; on treatment with cholesterol additional peaks were observed which arise from the crystallinity of cholesterol.

We performed cyclic voltammetric studies of the prepared gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode to understand its electrochemical behavior in presence of various bioanalytes. Prior to this, various experimental parameters (concentration, scan rate) were optimized inorder to define the working conditions.

The concentration of Au@CD nanoconjugates was varied in the first experiment to determine the optimized concentration that provided higher anodic and cathodic current. Fig. 2 (a) shows the electrochemical response with varying concentration of Au@CD nanoconjugates. The CV analysis was performed in the potential range of −0.05 to 0.5 V. As revealed from Fig. 2 (a), the electrochemical response was maximum at nanoconjugate concentration of 0.2% (w/v). The inset in Fig. 2(a), shows a systematic increase in cathodic current, Ic with increasing nanoconjugate, with highest current at 0.2% (w/v) concentration. The anodic current, Ia showed just the reverse behavior. From this data we concluded that with the nanoconjugate concentration of 0.2% (w/v), a maximum current or corresponding sensitivity was obtained and hence all further experiments were performed at this particular concentration of nanoconjugates.

In addition, we also performed the CV analysis of bare ITO and gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode. The analysis was performed at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. As observed in Figure S1 (Supplementary Information), a reduction in peak current was found in prepared electrode compared to bare ITO, which confirmed the presence of a film on the ITO surface. This further suggests a reduction in the mobility of charge carriers and corresponding mobility [16,17].

Thereafter, we investigated the effect of scan rate on the electrochemical current response in order to determine the nature of charge carrier transport process. Fig. 2(b) shows the electrochemical responses of gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode in the potential range of −0.05 to 0.5 V at a scan rate of 10–100 mV/s. We observed an increase in anodic and cathodic peak current with increasing square root of scan rate which suggests that the process is a diffusion controlled reaction. The inset in Fig. 2(b) shows the anodic and cathodic peak current as a function of square root of scan rate and showed a χ2 value 0.98.

In our earlier studies [13], we had reported the high selective detection of cholesterol using Au@CD nanoconjugates. As an extension to that, we fabricated the current (gelatin/Au@CD ITO) electrode system to allow for the electrochemical detection of cholesterol. An increase in peak current was observed with cholesterol concentration. We found that after addition of 2 mM cholesterol the anodic peak current almost doubled in comparison to that in absence of cholesterol (Fig. 2 (c) and (d)). Fig. 2 (c) illustrates a linear increase in peak current with the successive addition of cholesterol. A linear relationship with a least-square fitting χ2 value of 0.96 and 0.94 was found in anodic and cathodic peak current respectively. The linear detection limit was found to be in the range of 2–20 mM cholesterol. The sensitivity of electrochemical detection of cholesterol was calculated from slope of the linear curve and was found to be 1.36 μA/mM/cm2.

Simultaneously, we analyzed the ability of the gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode for other bioanalytes in a same concentration range of 2–20 mM at a fixed scan rate of 50 mV/s. No major changes were observed in the case of other tested biomolecules, except for oxalic acid. In case of oxalic acid changes were observed up to 6 mM concentration, which reached a steady value thereafter. Reports suggest that electrochemical sensors developed using polypyrrole matrix showed linearity up to a small concentration of 0.35 mM [3]. Some other sensors had a linearity beyond 0.35 mM however, these had a very high response time of >13 s [18,19]. Therefore, it is noteworthy to mention that the gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode in the present study showed a wide linear current response to cholesterol (2–20 mM) with a short response time of approximately 5 s, which is much lower than reported for chitosan hybride composites (13 s) [20], polypyrrole-hydrogen (30 s) [21] and Chox in a sol-gel films (51s) [22].

We have already proposed the mechanism behind the selective detection of cholesterol via Au@CD in our previous report [13]. The hydroxyl groups of cholesterol interact with the Au@CD functional groups (–OH and –COO- groups) that result in precipitation and corresponding changes in the optical and fluorescence properties. The prepared Au@CDs nanoconjugates are surface functionalized via free –OH and –COO- groups of citric acid and PEG. On addition of cholesterol, these free –COO- groups interact with the hydroxyl (-OH) groups of cholesterol resulting in interaction and corresponding precipitation of nanoconjugates. Cross-linking of particles results in aggregation which is assisted by visible changes in optical and fluorescence properties. Therefore, interaction of cholesterol with nanoconjugate part of the developed ITO electrode results in change in signal that is reflected in the anodic and cathodic peak current upon CV analysis. A graphical representation of the process mechanism has been depicted in Fig. 3 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of nitrogen (N2 +) beam implantation on cholesterol detection
ce:section.ce:para: To study the effect of irradiation on the electrochemical sensing profile of gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode, the ITO slides were subjected to nitrogen molecular ion (N2 +) beam at 50 KeV with fluences of 1012, 5 × 1012, 1013, 5 × 1013, 1014, 5 × 1014, 1015, 5 × 1015 and 1016 ions/cm2. The irradiated electrodes were then investigated for electrochemical responses in the potential range of 0.05–0.5 V. Fig. 4 shows the electrochemical signals of the irradiated gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode after subjecting to various fluences.

Fig. 4 shows the effect of irradiation on the electrochemical response of electrodes. We observed a gradual decrease in the anodic and cathodic peak current with increasing fluence up to N2 +fluence of 1015 ions/cm2. However, an increase in peak current was seen for the gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode with a further high fluence of 5 × 1015 and 1016 ions/cm2. The N2 + ions irradiation resulted in the incorporation of ions in the gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode treated with a high fluence. Implantation of ions results in changes in electron transfer occurring on the electrode surface which is the main reason behind the change in the magnitude of peak current after irradiation.

Electrochemical analysis of the irradiated electrode with cholecterol suggested significant changes in the magnitude of peak current. Cholesterol was used in the concentration range of 2–20 mM. Fig. 4 (c) and (d) shows the anodic and cathodic peak current of the irradiated electrodes in presence of cholesterol. An increase in peak current was seen with increasing cholesterol concentration in all the electrodes irradiated with different fluence. Maximum increase in peak current was observed in electrodes irradiated with high fluence, suggesting faster electron transfer between the electrode and electrolyte.

We also determined the sensitivity of the sensor. Sensitivity is defined as the sensor output per unit volume of the designated electrode towards the analyte and is determined from the slope of the plot. The sensitivity of the gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode irradiated with different fluence is shown in Fig. 5 , where we found a gradual decrease in sensitivity for the electrodes irradiated in the range of 1012 to 5 × 1016 ions/cm2. However maximum sensitivity was seen for gelatin/Au@CDs Ito electrodes irradiated with 1016 ions/cm2. Probably, irradiation with high fluence results in the breakage of hydrogen bonds thereby providing easy accessibility to active sites responsible for interaction with cholesterol. This results in high transfer of electrolytes to the electrode and the resultant increase in sensitivity.

The changes in peak magnitude after irradiation of the electrodes with N2 + ion beam suggested that, irradiation bring about morphological changes in the gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to analyze the changes brought about by irradiation (Fig. 5). SEM micrographs showed irregular flakes like structure on treatment with cholesterol (Fig. 5 (b)) the matrix prepared gelatin/Au@CD ITO electrode was almost regularly distributed. The occurrence of highly irregular flakes structure on the surface of gelatin/Au@CD ITO electrode confirmed the interaction of cholesterol with the electrode. With increasingfluence, slight distortion in the surface of the electrode matrix was seen suggesting that irradiation results in the removal of hydrogen bonds, providing more active sites for reaction with cholesterol. Fig. 5 (d) and (e) show the assembly of cholesterol in form of irregular flakes on the surface of irradiated electrodes. The analysis thus concluded that irradiation of the gelatin/Au@CD-ITO electrode results in alteration of the surface morphology providing better interaction with cholesterol for electrodes irradiated with high ion fluence.

The effective cholesterol biosensor can revolutionize the clinical analysis of a large number of diseases, such as cardiovascular diseases, hypothyroidism, nephrotic syndrome, diabetes, and liver diseases. In summary, we have designed a novel cholesterol electrochemical sensor using gelatin and Au@CD nanoconjugates. The developed biosensor (gelatin-Au@CD/ITO) exhibited high selectivity, sensitivity with a very rapid response towards cholesterol. They showed sensitivity of 1.36 μA mM−1 cm−2in the linear range of 2–20 mM with a correlation coefficient of 0.95. Additionally, irradiation with N2 + ion beam showed an enhancement in sensitivity when irradiated with fluence of 1016 ions/cm2. We anticipate that the current methodology that allows convenient and feasible cholesterol detection would extend new avenues for development of similar types of nanoconjugates based sensor. We intend to use this methodology to create miniaturized, transportable to the point of care or useable at-home biosensors for cholesterol monitoring in the future. Patients and hospitals will benefit from a better, automated lab-on-chip cholesterol biosensor that is quicker, more efficient, requires fewer samples, and costs less.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Authors declare no conflict of Interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: KR acknowledges ICMR (35/11/2019-Nano/BMS) and UGC-startup, Government of India, for research grant. The authors thank Advanced Instrument Research Facility (AIRF) of the University for TEM, SEM and FTIR facility. We are thankful to the Inter University Accelerator Centre (IUAC) for nitrogen ion beam irradiation facility. The authors are thankful to Dr. K. Asokan, IUAC for useful discussions.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Role of cholesterol and lipid organization in disease

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A cholesterol biosensor based on a bi-enzyme immobilized on conducting poly (thionine) film

Li, Amperometric third-generation hydrogen peroxide biosensor based on the immobilization of hemoglobin on multiwall carbon nanotubes and gold colloidal nanoparticles. Biosens. Bioelectron. 22(7) (2007) 1268-1274.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct electron transfer of cytochrome c and its biosensor based on gold nanoparticles/room temperature ionic liquid/carbon nanotubes composite film

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Xiang, Y. Zou, L.X. Sun, and F. Xu, Direct electron transfer of cytochrome c and its biosensor based on gold nanoparticles/room temperature ionic liquid/carbon nanotubes composite film. Electrochemistry Communications, 10(1) (2008) 38-41.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Au@carbon dot nanoconjugates as dual mode enzyme-free sensing platform for Cholesterol
